source.title: The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity
source.summary: By the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of all productive land was waqf property: irrevocable charitable endowments funding free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure.
source.author: Islamic Economics
source.body_text: By the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of all productive land was waqf property: irrevocable charitable endowments funding free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure. The Suleymaniye Complex, Al-Azhar University, and countless others operated for centuries on this principle. Then European colonial powers systematically dismantled it. This is the story of institutional memory, loss, and potential recovery. Topics: Waqf Endowments, Al-Azhar University, Ottoman Empire, Colonial Dismantling, Cash Waqf Revival Series: Historical Islamic Economics (Part I) • Episode 6 of 6

thread: Episode 6: “The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity.” This episode traces how waqf endowments supported public services across centuries. Read/listen: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6
At the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of productive land was waqf property, funding hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water works.
The episode examines institutions such as Al-Azhar and the Suleymaniye Complex, and how colonial-era policies disrupted waqf structures and institutional memory.
Listen for discussions on loss and potential recovery of waqf practices. Read/listen at the source: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6 #Waqf #OttomanHistory

linkedin.post: Podcast: “The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity” (Episode 6, Historical Islamic Economics). This episode reviews how waqf endowments became major institutional funders—supporting hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure—and how colonial interventions dismantled many waqf structures. It highlights examples such as Al-Azhar and the Suleymaniye Complex, and discusses themes of institutional memory and possibilities for recovery. Read and listen at the source: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6

Slide 4: Disruption: Colonial policies systematically dismantled many waqf institutions.

short_video.title: The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity
short_video.hook: How did charitable endowments run hospitals, universities and water systems for centuries?
short_video.voiceover: Waqf were irrevocable charitable endowments that funded public services across the Islamic world. At the Ottoman peak, around one-third of productive land was waqf property, supporting hospitals, universities like Al‑Azhar, soup kitchens and waterworks. The episode traces how colonial interventions dismantled many waqf structures and considers lessons for recovery. Read and listen at the source: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6
